title: Rotational symmetry of 2D shapes
description: Identify rotational symmetry in figures by determining magnitudes of rotations which map a figure onto itself.

assessment_items.question_content:
The center of the regular nonagon below is at the origin.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following rotational symmetries apply to the regular nonagon?**

Rotation | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Rotational symmetry of $40^\circ$ about the origin | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Rotational symmetry of $200^\circ$ about the origin | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

Will the nonagon map onto itself with $40^\circ$ and $200^\circ$ rotations about the origin?

Let's check the rotation of $40^\circ$ about the origin. There are $9$ congruent sides, and the nonagon centers at the origin.

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{9} = 40^\circ$

The figure maps onto itself, so it is **symmetric** about the origin at a $40^\circ$ magnitude of rotation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Every additional rotation of $40^\circ$ about the origin that sums to less than $360^\circ$ represents another valid magnitude of rotational symmetry for the figure.  *Every* figure maps onto itself if it turns a full circle, so we do not call this a kind of symmetry.

The regular nonagon has rotational symmetry around the origin at magnitudes of:

- $40^\circ$
- $80^\circ$
- $120^\circ$
- $160^\circ$
- $200^\circ$
- $240^\circ$
- $280^\circ$
- $320^\circ$

This is the completed table:

Rotation | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Rotational symmetry of $40^\circ$ about the origin | Yes
Rotational symmetry of $200^\circ$ about the origin | Yes

options.alt: A nonagon mapped onto a coordinate plane. The center is at the origin. The nonagon has a point at zero, seven, a point at five, five, a point at seven, one, a point at six, negative four, a point a two, negative seven, a point at negative three, negative six point five, a point at negative six point five, negative three, a point at negative seven, two, and a point at negative four point two, six.

assessment_items.question_content:
The center of the star below is at the origin. The star has $5$ congruent acute angles that alternate with $5$ congruent reflex angles. The sides are all congruent.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following rotational symmetries apply to the star?**

Rotation | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Rotational symmetry of $62^\circ$ about the origin | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Rotational symmetry of $288^\circ$ about the origin | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

Will the star map onto itself with $62^\circ$ and $288^\circ$ rotations about the origin?

There are $5$ congruent angles at the points of the star, and the star centers at the origin.

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{5} = 72^\circ$

When we rotate the star $72^\circ$ about the origin, the figure maps onto itself, so it is **symmetric** about the origin at a $72^\circ$ magnitude of rotation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Every additional rotation of $72^\circ$ about the origin that sums to less than $360^\circ$ represents another valid magnitude of rotational symmetry for the figure.  *Every* figure maps onto itself if it turns a full circle, so we do not call this a kind of symmetry.

The star has rotational symmetry around the origin at magnitudes of:

- $1 \cdot 72 \degree =72^\circ$
- $2 \cdot 72 \degree =144^\circ$
- $3 \cdot 72 \degree =216^\circ$
- $4 \cdot 72 \degree =288^\circ$

The star does **not** have rotational symmetry at a magnitude of $62^\circ$.



[[☃ image 1]]

This is the completed table:

Rotation | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Rotational symmetry of $62^\circ$ about the origin | No
Rotational symmetry of $288^\circ$ about the origin | Yes

options.alt: A star with ten sides mapped onto a coordinate plane. The center of the star is at the origin. The star has a point at zero, eight point five, a point at one point eight, two point three, a point at eight point five, three point eight, a point at three, negative one, a point at five point five, negative seven point five, a point at zero, negative three, a point at negative five point five, negative seven point five, a point at negative three, negative one, a point at negative eight point five, three point eight, and a point at negative one point eight, two point three.

assessment_items.question_content:

[[☃ image 1]]

hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

Will the hexagon map onto itself with $60^\circ$ and $120^\circ$ rotations about the origin?

Let's check the rotation of $60^\circ$ about the origin. There are $6$ congruent sides, and the hexagon centers at the origin.

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{6} = 60^\circ$

The figure maps onto itself, so it is **symmetric** about the origin at a $60^\circ$ magnitude of rotation.



[[☃ image 1]]

Every additional rotation of $60^\circ$ about the origin that sums to less than $360^\circ$ represents another valid magnitude of rotational symmetry for the figure.  *Every* figure maps onto itself if it turns a full circle, so we do not call this a kind of symmetry.

The regular hexagon has rotational symmetry around the origin at magnitudes of:

- $60^\circ$
- $120^\circ$
- $180^\circ$
- $240^\circ$
- $300^\circ$

This is the completed table:

Rotation | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Rotational symmetry of $60^\circ$ about the origin | Yes
Rotational symmetry of $120^\circ$ about the origin | Yes

options.alt: A hexagon mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The center is at the origin. The hexagon has a point around negative seven, one half, a point around seven, negative one, a point around negative three, six and a half, a point around three, negative six and a half, a point around four, six, and a point around negative four, negative six. All points are estimates.

assessment_items.question_content:
The figure below is a kite.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following rotational symmetries apply to the kite?**

Rotation | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Rotational symmetry of $90^\circ$ about the origin | [[☃ dropdown 1]]
Rotational symmetry of $180^\circ$ about the origin | [[☃ dropdown 2]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

Will the kite map onto itself with $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$ rotations about the origin?

Because this figure has $2$ angles which have unique measurements, the figure cannot have rotational symmetry.

When we check the rotations of $90^\circ$ and $180^\circ$, some points on the kite map to points which are not on the figure. So the kite does **not** have rotational symmetry.



[[☃ image 1]]



[[☃ image 2]]

This is the completed table:

Rotation | Applies to the figure?
:- | :-:
Rotational symmetry of $90^\circ$ about the origin | No
Rotational symmetry of $180^\circ$ about the origin | No

options.alt: A kite mapped onto a coordinate plane. The x- y-coordinate plane scales by ones. The kite has a point at negative six, negative six, a point at negative five, zero, a point at zero, negative five, and a point at eight, eight.

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's check the rotation of $180^\circ$ about the origin.

The figure maps onto itself, so it is **symmetric** about the origin at a $180^\circ$ magnitude of rotation.

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are magnitudes for rotational symmetry of the rectangle below about the marked point?**


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

A rectangle has $2$ pairs of congruent line segments.  Thus if it repeats at all, it must repeat $2$ times in a full $360^\circ$ rotation.

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{2} = 180^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]

We see that a $180^\circ$ rotation would map every point of the rectangle to itself.  [[☃ explanation 1]]
The rectangle has rotational symmetry about the point for a magnitude of $180^\circ$.

options.alt: A rectangle with a point in the center.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are magnitudes for rotational symmetry of the isosceles trapezoid below about the marked point?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

Because this trapezoid has $2$ sides which have unique lengths, the figure cannot have rotational symmetry.



[[☃ image 1]]
The isosceles trapezoid does not have rotational symmetry.

options.alt: A trapezoid with a point in its center. Two sides of the trapezoid are parallel. The other two side of the trapezoid are congruent.

choices.content: The isosceles trapezoid does not have rotational symmetry.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are magnitudes for rotational symmetry of the hexagon below about the marked point?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

The hexagon has $6$ congruent line segments, but two sets of $3$ congruent angles.  Thus if it repeats at all, it must repeat $3$ times in a full $360^\circ$ rotation.

[Do I need an explanation here proving that the other 3 angles are congruent to each other?]:

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{3} = 120^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]

We see that a $120^\circ$ rotation would map every point of the hexagon to itself. What other rotations map the hexagon to itself? [[☃ explanation 1]]

Every additional rotation of $120^\circ$ that sums to less than $360^\circ$ represents another valid magnitude of rotational symmetry for the figure. *Every* figure maps onto itself if it turns a full circle, so we do not call this a kind of symmetry.

This hexagon has rotational symmetry around the center at magnitudes of:

- $120^\circ$
- $240^\circ$
The hexagon has rotational symmetry about the point for a magnitude of $120^\circ$ and $240^\circ$.

options.alt: A hexagon with a point in the center.

[[☃ image 1]]

A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

Because this figure has $2$ angles which have unique measurements, the figure cannot have rotational symmetry.



[[☃ image 1]]
The quadrilateral does not have rotational symmetry.

options.alt: A quadrilateral with a point in the center.

choices.content: The quadrilateral does not have rotational symmetry.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are magnitudes for rotational symmetry of the circle below about its center?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

By definition, every point in a circle is an equal distance from the center.

Since the circle includes points a full $360^\circ$ around the center, the circle has **every magnitude** of rotational symmetry.
Of the choices, the circle has rotational symmetry about its center for magnitudes of $7^\circ$, $45^\circ$, $90^\circ$, $210^\circ$, and $355^\circ$.

options.alt: A circle with a point in the center.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are magnitudes for rotational symmetry of the ellipse below about its center?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

An ellipse has $2$ points which are at a greater distance from the center than all other points on the ellipse.  Thus if it repeats at all, it must repeat $2$ times in a full $360^\circ$ rotation.

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{2} = 180^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]

We see that a $180^\circ$ rotation would map every point of the ellipse to itself.  [[☃ explanation 1]]
The ellipse has rotational symmetry about its center for a magnitude of $180^\circ$.

options.alt: An ellipse with a point in the center.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are magnitudes for rotational symmetry of the regular pentagon below about its center?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

A regular pentagon has $5$ congruent line segments and $5$ congruent angles.  Thus if it repeats at all, it must repeat $5$ times in a full $360^\circ$ rotation.

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{5} = 72^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]

We see that a $72^\circ$ rotation would map every point of the pentagon to itself.  What other rotations map the pentagon to itself?  [[☃ explanation 1]]

Every additional rotation of $72^\circ$ that sums to less than $360^\circ$ represents another valid magnitude of rotational symmetry for the figure. *Every* figure maps onto itself if it turns a full circle, so we do not call this a kind of symmetry.

The regular pentagon has rotational symmetry around the center at magnitudes of:

- $72^\circ$
- $144^\circ$
- $216^\circ$
- $288^\circ$
Of the choices available, the regular pentagon has rotational symmetry about its center for a magnitude of $72^\circ$, $144^\circ$, and $216^\circ$.

options.alt: A pentagon with a point in the center.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are magnitudes for rotational symmetry of the square below about the marked point?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

A square has $4$ congruent line segments and $4$ congruent angles.  Thus if it repeats at all, it must repeat $4$ times in a full $360^\circ$ rotation.

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{4} = 90^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]

We see that a $90^\circ$ rotation would map every point of the square to itself.  What other rotations map the square to itself?  [[☃ explanation 1]]

Every additional rotation of $90^\circ$ that sums to less than $360^\circ$ represents another valid magnitude of rotational symmetry for the figure. *Every* figure maps onto itself if it turns a full circle, so we do not call this a kind of symmetry.

The square has rotational symmetry around the center at magnitudes of:

- $90^\circ$
- $180^\circ$
- $270^\circ$
Of the choices available, the square has rotational symmetry about the point for a magnitude of $180^\circ$ and $270^\circ$.

options.alt: A square with a point in the center.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are magnitudes for rotational symmetry of the regular octagon below about the marked point?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

A regular octagon has $8$ congruent line segments and $8$ congruent angles.  Thus if it repeats at all, it must repeat $8$ times in a full $360^\circ$ rotation.

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{8} = 45^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]

We see that a $45^\circ$ rotation would map every point of the octagon to itself.  What other rotations map the octagon to itself?  [[☃ explanation 1]]

Every additional rotation of $45^\circ$ that sums to less than $360^\circ$ represents another valid magnitude of rotational symmetry for the figure. *Every* figure maps onto itself if it turns a full circle, so we do not call this a kind of symmetry.

The regular octagon has rotational symmetry around the center at magnitudes of:

- $45^\circ$
- $90^\circ$
- $135^\circ$
- $180^\circ$
- $225^\circ$
- $270^\circ$
- $315^\circ$
Of the choices available, the regular octagon has rotational symmetry about the point for a magnitude of $45^\circ$, $180^\circ$, and $225^\circ$.

options.alt: A octagon with a point in the center.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following are magnitudes for rotational symmetry of the parallelogram below about the marked point?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A figure has **rotational symmetry** if there is a rotation that maps the figure onto itself.

Any line segment in the figure would have to map onto a congruent line segment in the same figure.  

Further, the segment lengths and angles between them must repeat in the same order around the center.

The **magnitude** of the rotation is the number of degrees we can rotate the figure to map it onto itself.

A parallelogram has $2$ pairs of congruent line segments.  Thus if it repeats at all, it must repeat $2$ times in a full $360^\circ$ rotation.

$\dfrac{360^\circ}{2} = 180^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]

We see that a $180^\circ$ rotation would map every point of the parallelogram to itself.  [[☃ explanation 1]]
The parallelogram has rotational symmetry about the point for a magnitude of $180^\circ$.

options.alt: A parallelogram with a point in the center.
